title: Dynamical Signatures of Floquet Topology in Wave Packet Dynamics
authors: Xin Shen, Bing Lu, Yan-Qing Zhu
abstract: Periodically driven quantum systems, known as Floquet systems, provide a versatile platform for engineering novel topological phases absent in static settings. However, dynamically characterizing these non-equilibrium topological invariants remains a challenge. Here, we develop a Floquet perturbation theory in the extended Hilbert space to analytically describe the center-of-mass (CoM) dynamics of a wave packet. When applied to the driven Su-Schrieffer-Heeger model, our theory reveals that the CoM exhibits multi-frequency Zitterbewegung oscillations, whose spectral composition and phase are directly tied to the system's Floquet band structure. Crucially, we find that band inversions at topological phase transitions imprint distinct signatures in the CoM dynamics, including the emergence of low-frequency modes and phase shifts of the oscillatory trajectory. These dynamical signatures offer a practical protocol for detecting Floquet topological invariants, which we demonstrate for both high-frequency and strongly driven regimes. Our work establishes CoM dynamics as a simple and experimentally accessible probe for exploring topological phase transitions in Floquet systems.
summary: This paper proposes using the center-of-mass oscillations of a wave packet to detect topological phase transitions in periodically driven systems. By analyzing the frequency and phase of Zitterbewegung-like motion, researchers can identify band inversions in Floquet systems. This method offers a simple and practical protocol for characterizing non-equilibrium topological phases in platforms like ultracold gases.
main_problem: The difficulty of dynamically characterizing non-equilibrium topological invariants in periodically driven (Floquet) systems.
main_result: The authors establish that the center-of-mass (CoM) dynamics of a wave packet exhibit multi-frequency Zitterbewegung oscillations that serve as a practical probe for detecting Floquet topological phase transitions and band inversions.
method: Development of a Floquet perturbation theory in an extended Hilbert space to analytically describe CoM dynamics and link them to the system's quasienergy band structure.
model_or_system: A periodically driven Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model, which is a 1D tight-binding lattice with time-periodic hopping parameters.
key_observables: Center-of-mass (CoM) position expectation value, Zitterbewegung oscillation frequencies, and Floquet winding numbers.
important_parameters: Driving amplitude (A), driving frequency (omega), hopping parameters (J1, J2), and wave packet width (d); regimes include high-frequency and strongly driven limits.
main_assumptions: The analysis focuses on stroboscopic dynamics, assumes a momentum-independent external drive, and utilizes a truncated first-order perturbative expansion valid for single-photon processes.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the phase diagram of the driven SSH model; Figure 2 compares numerical CoM evolution with perturbative approximations; Figure 3 illustrates quasienergy spectra and CoM trajectories during frequency- and amplitude-induced phase transitions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of Floquet topological characterization, develops a new perturbation theory in extended Hilbert space, applies this theory to the driven SSH model to derive CoM dynamics, validates the theory with numerical simulations, and demonstrates the detection of topological phase transitions.
why_interesting: It provides a direct, experimentally accessible link between observable quantum dynamics (wave packet motion) and abstract topological invariants, which is highly relevant for characterizing driven states in ultracold atom and photonic platforms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Driving induced Floquet topological phase transition. The chiral invariants are calculated through Eq. (40) for J1 = 1 and J2 = 1.5. J1 is taken as the unit for both horizontal and vertical axes.

FIG. 2. The evolution of the wave packet’s center of mass governed by the Hamiltonian Eq. (38). The solid lines repre- sent exact numerical solutions obtained by directly calculating the time-dependent Schr¨odinger’s equation, while the dashed lines correspond to results from the first-order perturbative approximation of the wavefunction. The dot-dashed curves depict the low-frequency component extracted from the first- order approximation. All calculations employ the parameters J1 = 1, J2 = 1.5, ω = 5.5, and the width of wave packet d = 10. Panels (a) and (b) respectively use A = 1 and A = 3 as the driving amplitude. The time is in units of 1/J1 and the length is in units of the lattice...

FIG. 3. (a) Typical quasienergy spectrum obtained via diag- onalization of the extended Hamiltonian. The dashed square and circles highlight the band inversion points within the π- gap and 0-gap regions, respectively. In panels (b) and (c), solid lines and dot-dashed curves represent the exact numer- ical solutions. In (b), the frequency-induced phase transi- tion is marked by the arrow in the right panel of Fig. 1. The dotted lines correspond to the low-frequency compo- nents calculated by Eq. (47).
